title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — July 10, 2026
headline: Tunisia's first individual review proposes a score of 23.8, down from a never-checked 34.4.
summary: Tunisia received its first individual assessment on July 10. Its published score of 34.4 out of 100 turned out to be a placeholder that no analyst had actually checked. The new evidence-based score is 23.8 out of 100, a drop of 10.6 points, but Tunisia stays in the same performance band, so this is not a full downgrade. This is only a proposal right now: the published score stays at 34.4 while the benchmark's editors review it. The finding rests on Tunisia sentencing Sihem Bensedrine, the former head of its own truth commission, to 25 years in prison, and on the mass expulsion of more than 12,000 sub-Saharan migrants, carried out with documented torture. In Gaza, Israel's territorial control grew from about 53 percent to nearly 70 percent during a nominal ceasefire, NPR reported July 10. Israel already scores 0 of 100, the lowest possible mark, so this cannot push its score down further, but the scale of the expansion is worth naming clearly. Yemen offered a rare piece of good news: the largest prisoner exchange in its history reportedly began July 10, though Yemen was not reassessed tonight. Venezuela's earthquake death toll held roughly flat at just over 3,800, the first time it has not climbed since the disaster began; its disaster-response score stays just under the point threshold that would trigger a public change. A long-running question about whether Mali and Burkina Faso are scored consistently for comparable conduct has now gone seventeen days without an answer from the benchmark's coordinating team.

pipeline.tunisiaHandling: Tunisia's proposed score of 23.8 of 100 (down from a published 34.4 that was never individually checked) is a proposal only. The published score remains 34.4 of 100 while the benchmark's editors review the finding. No change has been made to the live index.

topSignals.title: Tunisia's First Individual Review Proposes a Score of 23.8, Down From a Never-Checked 34.4
topSignals.whyItMatters: Tunisia's published score was a placeholder that had never actually been checked against real evidence. The new reading is 10.6 points lower, though the published score won't change until reviewers decide.
topSignals.description: Tunisia holds a published score of 34.4 of 100 in the Developing band. That number was a placeholder from the benchmark's original bulk data import -- it had never been checked against Tunisia's own record. Tonight's first individual review of Tunisia proposes a score of 23.8 of 100, a drop of 10.6 points, though Tunisia stays within the same Developing band. The published score of 34.4 remains in place while the benchmark's editors review the proposal. The finding rests on three things. First, on July 8, a Tunisian court sentenced Sihem Bensedrine -- the former president of Tunisia's own Truth and Dignity Commission, the body set up after the 2011 revolution to investigate past abuses -- to 25 years in prison, which the review treats as the government criminalizing its own past effort at accountability. Second, Tunisia suspended access to asylum and collectively expelled more than 12,000 sub-Saharan migrants between January and April 2025, in conditions Amnesty International documented as including torture and sexual violence. Third, mass trials on vague conspiracy charges have produced sentences of up to 66 years, and Tunisia shut down its independent information-access authority in August 2025. The proposed 23.8 stays above Turkey (17.6 of 100) and China (19.5 of 100), two countries the benchmark already scores lower for similar patterns of dismantling independent institutions -- a comparison the review calls consistent with Tunisia's smaller scale of repression and larger remaining civil-liberties space. Confidence: medium, because this is a first review with no prior individual baseline for comparison.

evidence.quote: Former truth commission president Sihem Bensedrine sentenced to 25 years in prison
evidence.claim: Tunisia is imprisoning the leader of its own past accountability body, reversing transitional justice.

evidence.quote: Sub-Saharan migrants collectively expelled to the Algerian and Libyan borders in life-threatening conditions
evidence.claim: Tunisia carried out mass expulsions of migrants amounting to a racially targeted pattern of harm.

evidence.quote: Dozens of opposition politicians and civil society figures subjected to mass trials on vague security-related charges
evidence.claim: Tunisia has systematically dismantled democratic and judicial institutions since the 2021 power grab.
evidence.source: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2026: Tunisia (Partly Free, 42/100)

topSignals.title: Israel's Territorial Control Over Gaza Grows to Nearly 70 Percent During the Ceasefire
topSignals.whyItMatters: Israel already holds the lowest possible score, so this expansion cannot push the score down any further -- but the size of the change is worth recording plainly.
topSignals.description: Israel holds a published score of 0 of 100, the lowest possible mark. Nine months into a nominal ceasefire, Israeli military control inside Gaza has grown from roughly 53 percent to nearly 70 percent of the territory, NPR reported July 10, including a new zone of control over the al-Shujaiya area. Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu described the expanding footprint as a deliberate, step-by-step plan to encircle Hamas. The United Nations humanitarian office says roughly 200 Palestinians have been killed by Israeli forces since the ceasefire began, and more than 1,000 total since it took effect. Because Israel's score is already at the floor, this cannot lower the score further -- the benchmark records it as confirming, and reinforcing, the existing floor score rather than as a new downgrade. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: NPR, July 10, 2026: nine months into the ceasefire, Israeli military territorial control inside Gaza has expanded from roughly 53 percent to nearly 70 percent, described by Prime Minister Netanyahu as a deliberate step-by-step plan.

topSignals.title: Sudan and Russia Both Hold at Zero as Documented Violence Against Civilians Continues
topSignals.whyItMatters: Both governments already hold the lowest possible score for ongoing harm to civilians. Tonight's evidence confirms that finding again, but cannot lower a score that is already at zero.
topSignals.description: Sudan holds at 0 of 100. United Nations investigators warned on July 9-10 that the city of El Obeid, where roughly 500,000 civilians and 105,000 displaced people are trapped, must not become the next mass-casualty site -- a warning that follows the UN's July 9 finding that mass killings and gang rapes by the Rapid Support Forces in El Fasher amount to genocide. Russia also holds at 0 of 100. Russia carried out mass-casualty strikes on Kyiv, Korosten, and Zaporizhzhia on July 10 that killed and injured civilians, including a child; the UN Security Council described a deeply alarming civilian toll from the past week. Both countries are already at the lowest possible score, so tonight's evidence confirms rather than lowers each score. Confidence: high for both.

evidence.claim: UN News, July 2026: investigators warned that El Obeid must not become the next crime scene, following the UN Fact-Finding Mission's genocide determination for El Fasher.

topSignals.title: Three African Countries Hold Near or At the Floor as Disease and Hunger Deepen
topSignals.whyItMatters: None of tonight's evidence is caused by government action, so it does not change any of the three scores -- but the human toll keeps climbing in all three places.
topSignals.description: The Democratic Republic of the Congo holds at 2.3 of 100, just above the absolute floor. Its Ebola outbreak reached 625 confirmed deaths and 1,792 cases as of July 10, up from 600 the night before, with two suspected cases in the city of Kisangani raising the possibility the outbreak is spreading geographically -- still the fastest-growing Ebola outbreak on record. Somalia holds at 4.7 of 100. The Burhakaba district in Somalia's Bay region has now surpassed the famine-level malnutrition threshold, with 6.5 million people facing high acute food insecurity nationwide. South Sudan holds at 0 of 100, the lowest possible score. Roughly 7.8 million people, more than half the country, face crisis-level or worse hunger, with 73,000 people in famine-level conditions in the Greater Jonglei and Upper Nile regions, where conflict -- including by government-aligned forces -- is blocking aid access. Ebola and drought are not caused by government action and do not move the Congo's or Somalia's scores; South Sudan's conflict-driven aid blockage is partly a government-attributable factor, but it is a continuation of an already-documented pattern, not a new escalation, so South Sudan's floor score is confirmed rather than newly justified. Confidence: high for all three.

evidence.claim: Al Jazeera, July 2026: confirmed Ebola deaths in the Democratic Republic of the Congo reached 625, with 1,792 total cases, as suspected cases emerged in Kisangani.
FAO, 2026: 7.8 million people in South Sudan face high acute food insecurity, and 2.2 million children suffer acute malnutrition.

topSignals.title: Venezuela's Earthquake Toll Levels Off as a 17-Day Scoring Question Stays Open
topSignals.whyItMatters: One disaster-response score stays just below the threshold for change, a long-standing consistency question remains unanswered, and one rare piece of good news awaits its own review.
topSignals.description: Venezuela holds at 18 of 100. Its earthquake death toll topped 3,800 as of day 17 (July 10) -- essentially flat compared with 3,899 the day before, the first time the toll has not visibly climbed since the disaster began. Recalculating Venezuela's disaster-response performance for this evidence would put the score at roughly 15.3, a decline of about 2.7 points, still short of the 5-point threshold the benchmark requires before changing a published score -- the same reduced reading confirmed for a fourth consecutive night. Separately, the open question of whether Mali (12.5 of 100) and Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) are scored consistently for comparable military-government conduct has now gone seventeen consecutive days without a decision from the benchmark's coordinating team -- the longest-running open item the benchmark is tracking. On a more hopeful note, Yemen reportedly began the largest prisoner exchange in its history on July 10, involving detainees held by the Houthi movement and other parties; Yemen was not reassessed tonight, and the exchange has not yet been independently verified or scored. Confidence: high for Venezuela; not yet assessed for Yemen.

evidence.claim: Democracy Now, July 10, 2026: Venezuela's earthquake death toll topped 3,800 amid a deepening public-health crisis.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Due-process restoration, civil-society reinstatement, and reversal of the indefinite re-election amendment remain the path back toward 20 of 100. The 2027 election under the amendment is the next scheduled test.
boundaryWatch.note: El Salvador sits at 15.0 of 100 in the Critical band, 5.0 points below the boundary with the Developing band. Not assessed tonight; fifth cycle since the July 5 downgrade was applied.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A confirmed enforcement action under the new Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad -- is the next threshold event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: China sits at 19.5 of 100, half a point inside the Critical band. Fresh political-prisoner actions this cycle (a Macao national-security arrest, a Tibet-rights editor's detention, a nine-year sentence for a 2022 banner protester) continue the priced pattern without a new discrete triggering event.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A second undisclosed telemetry episode, evidence the removed tracker was broader or retained longer than stated, or a regulator or court finding of deceptive practice would each independently move the score toward a change. The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable August 2.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits at 59.1 of 100, 0.9 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight. A separate identity-verification policy change, a source-code leak, and a trademark dispute surfaced this cycle but none meet the specified conversion trigger for this watch.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A labor board ruling on the unfair-labor-practice charge tied to Apple's closure of its first unionized store is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits at 59.4 of 100, 0.6 points below the Established band. The labor board review has not yet concluded. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A durable ceasefire combined with the Lebanese Armed Forces taking sovereign control of southern Lebanon and delivering reconstruction would together move Lebanon toward 20 of 100.
boundaryWatch.note: Lebanon sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the boundary with the Developing band. The main source of harm within the window remains the Israel-Hezbollah conflict, not Lebanon's own conduct.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently verified deliberate targeting of civilians in a cross-border strike would move Pakistan's score down; durable relief for detained protesters and disappeared Balochistan residents would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Pakistan sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the Developing band. Balochistan military operations escalated this cycle, with about 75 people killed since July 7, reinforcing rather than deepening the existing pattern.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independent confirmation of the forensic pathologist's undercount allegation, or documented aid obstruction, is the next event that would move Venezuela's score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Venezuela sits at 18.0 of 100. Day 17 earthquake response evidence would move the score to roughly 15.3 if applied -- a 2.7-point decline, unchanged for a fourth consecutive check. The death toll leveled off this cycle for the first time since the disaster began.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration in Borno state combined with documented aid obstruction is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Nigeria sits at 18.0 of 100, 2.0 points below the Developing band. Northern hunger remains the worst in nearly a decade, continuing the already-priced June 19 downgrade.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization response corridors would be the trigger to move toward the absolute floor. The natural seasonal peak for this outbreak runs through July 31.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC sits at 2.3 of 100, 2.3 points above the absolute floor. Its Ebola outbreak passed 625 deaths this cycle, with suspected spread to Kisangani.

boundaryWatch.trigger: US-Iran talks resume July 11 in Doha. A documented lethal crackdown on political rivals would move Iran's score toward the floor.
boundaryWatch.note: Iran sits at 2.5 of 100, 2.5 points above the absolute floor. Not assessed tonight; talks with the United States are scheduled to resume tomorrow.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented expulsions of Bengali Muslim and Rohingya populations, and continued digital surveillance targeting minority groups, remain under watch. Not assessed tonight.
boundaryWatch.note: India sits at 15.6 of 100, 0.6 points above the absolute floor, in its twenty-sixth consecutive monitoring cycle. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration covering Buur Hakaba or Baidoa, combined with documented government aid obstruction, is the next scored trigger. The famine-risk window runs through September 2026.
boundaryWatch.note: Somalia sits at 4.7 of 100. The Burhakaba district passed the famine-level malnutrition threshold this cycle. Seventeenth floor-proximity cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented discriminatory denial of emergency protection to stateless bidoon or migrant workers during wartime would move Kuwait's score down; durable expansion of bidoon rights would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Kuwait sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight; twelfth boundary-watch cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Mali's conduct against Burkina Faso's (6.3 of 100) is the next scored event needed to resolve this gap. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali sits at 12.5 of 100. The calibration question against peer country Burkina Faso has now been open seventeen consecutive days, with no resolution timeline set.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Burkina Faso's conduct against Mali's (12.5 of 100) is the next scored event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Burkina Faso sits at 6.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight; the calibration question against Mali remains open seventeen consecutive days.

boundaryWatch.trigger: The outcome of the Justice Department's False Claims Act complaint or the Barrows v. Humana class action over AI-driven claim denials would confirm the current Developing-band placement either way.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana sits at 35.2 of 100, 4.8 points below the Functional band. Not assessed tonight; neither legal matter has a fixed hearing date.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Forced cuts that measurably reduce student or staff support would count against the score; abandoning its academic-freedom stance would as well.
boundaryWatch.note: Princeton sits at 57.8 of 100, 2.2 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently documented deliberate targeting of Palestinian civilians by Palestinian authorities themselves, or systematic diversion of aid, would move the score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Palestine sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented atrocities by Haitian state security forces, or deliberate relief obstruction, would move the score down. Verified state-led civilian protection would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Haiti sits at 4.7 of 100. Temporary Protected Status work authorization for roughly 350,000 Haitians in the United States expired today, July 10 -- but that is US conduct, not scored against Haiti.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A fourth nationwide blackout, or a documented healthcare-access failure beyond tens of thousands of canceled surgeries, is the next event that would move Cuba's score toward a formal change.
boundaryWatch.note: Cuba sits at 32.8 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A discrete new harm category beyond documented opposition and press suppression is the next event that would move Turkey's score further down.
boundaryWatch.note: Turkey sits at 10.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight. Tunisia's proposed 23.8 of 100 stays well above Turkey's published score for a related pattern of dismantling independent institutions.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A return to lethal crackdown tactics, such as the 41 protester deaths recorded during the 2025 Saba Saba march, would be the next event that moves Kenya's score down toward the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Kenya sits at 35.9 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented own-conduct deterioration in epidemic response, such as government obstruction of WHO response corridors, would be the next event that could push Uganda back into the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Uganda sits at 20.3 of 100, just 0.3 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A merits ruling or verdict for the states, a material settlement, or new dated evidence of youth-safety harm or internal-findings suppression is the next event that would move Meta's score.
boundaryWatch.note: Meta Platforms sits at 7.8 of 100, near the bottom of the Fortune 500 list. Not assessed tonight.

emergingRisks.risk: Tunisia's first individual review finds its published score was a placeholder overstated by 10.6 points, raising the question of whether other never-individually-checked country scores carry similar gaps.
emergingRisks.detail: The proposed 23.8 of 100 stays within the Developing band and is not yet applied to the published record. The next event to watch is whether the benchmark's editors act on the proposal, and whether any other placeholder-era country scores warrant an earlier individual check.

emergingRisks.risk: Israel's territorial control inside Gaza has grown from about 53 percent to nearly 70 percent during the ceasefire, a scale of change the lowest possible score cannot reflect with a further point drop.
emergingRisks.detail: Israel already holds 0 of 100. The benchmark records this expansion as reinforcing, not lowering, that score, since there is no lower number available.

emergingRisks.risk: The Mali-Burkina Faso scoring question has now run seventeen consecutive days without a decision from the benchmark's coordinating team.
emergingRisks.detail: The question is whether Mali (12.5 of 100) and Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100), two military governments with comparably documented failures to protect civilians, are being scored consistently against each other. It remains the longest-open question the benchmark is tracking.

emergingRisks.risk: Venezuela's earthquake-response score has held at a roughly 2.7-point sub-threshold decline for a fourth consecutive check, even as the death toll levels off for the first time.
emergingRisks.detail: Independent verification of a forensic pathologist's claim that the true death toll is far higher than reported would be the event that moves this from a documented watch to a scored change.

emergingRisks.risk: Three African countries face compounding, non-government-caused crises at once: Congo's Ebola outbreak past 625 deaths, Somalia's famine-level malnutrition in Burhakaba, and South Sudan's conflict-blocked famine response.
emergingRisks.detail: None of these is scored as government-caused harm on its own, but continued deterioration without a matching government response could shift the analysis for any of the three countries in a future cycle.

emergingRisks.risk: Yemen's reported largest-ever prisoner exchange, which began July 10, has not yet been independently verified or individually assessed.
emergingRisks.detail: If verified, this would be one of the few clearly positive-direction developments recorded this year. Yemen was not in tonight's assessed cluster.

sectorAlerts.sector: North Africa -- Tunisia's First Individual Review Proposes a 10.6-Point Correction
sectorAlerts.headline: Tunisia's published 34.4 of 100 was a never-checked placeholder; the first individual review proposes 23.8 of 100, still under review.
observations: Tunisia (34.4 of 100 published; 23.8 of 100 proposed): the drop is driven mainly by accountability and equity failures, including a 25-year prison sentence for the former head of Tunisia's own truth commission and the mass expulsion of over 12,000 migrants.
The proposed score stays within the Developing band and above Critical-band peers Turkey (17.6 of 100) and China (19.5 of 100).
The published score remains 34.4 of 100 while the benchmark's editors review the proposal; no change has been made to the live record.

sectorAlerts.sector: Middle East -- Israel's Ceasefire-Era Territorial Expansion
sectorAlerts.headline: Israeli control inside Gaza grows from about 53 percent to nearly 70 percent during the ceasefire; the score holds at 0 of 100, the lowest possible mark.
observations: Israel (0 of 100): Prime Minister Netanyahu described the expanding footprint as a deliberate step-by-step plan to encircle Hamas.
The United Nations humanitarian office reports roughly 200 Palestinians killed by Israeli forces since the ceasefire began.
Because Israel already holds the lowest possible score, the finding is recorded as reinforcing rather than lowering the score.

sectorAlerts.sector: Countries at the Floor -- Sudan and Russia Confirmed Again
sectorAlerts.headline: UN investigators warn of a second El Fasher-style siege at El Obeid, while Russia's July 10 strikes on Ukraine add to an already-floored record.
observations: Sudan (0 of 100): the RSF's encirclement of El Obeid, trapping roughly 500,000 civilians, follows the UN's July 9 genocide finding for El Fasher.
Russia (0 of 100): strikes on Kyiv, Korosten, and Zaporizhzhia on July 10 killed and injured civilians, including a child.
Both countries already hold the lowest possible score, so tonight's evidence confirms rather than lowers each score.

sectorAlerts.headline: Congo's Ebola outbreak passes 625 deaths, Somalia's Burhakaba district crosses the famine threshold, and South Sudan's conflict blocks aid to 73,000 people at catastrophe level.
observations: Democratic Republic of the Congo (2.3 of 100): 625 confirmed Ebola deaths and 1,792 cases as of July 10, with suspected spread to Kisangani.
Somalia (4.7 of 100): the Burhakaba district in Bay region has surpassed the famine-level malnutrition threshold.
South Sudan (0 of 100): roughly 7.8 million people face crisis-level hunger, with conflict -- including by government-aligned forces -- blocking aid access to 73,000 people at catastrophe level.

sectorAlerts.sector: Latin America -- Venezuela's Earthquake Response Levels Off
sectorAlerts.headline: Venezuela's earthquake death toll holds near 3,800 for the first time since the disaster began, as the disaster-response score stays under the change threshold for a fourth check.
observations: Venezuela (18.0 of 100): the death toll topped 3,800 as of day 17, essentially unchanged from 3,899 the day before.
Recalculating for this evidence would move the score to roughly 15.3, a 2.7-point decline -- still under the 5-point threshold for a change.
This remains the leading candidate for a formal score change if the undercount allegation is independently verified.

sectorAlerts.sector: Sahel -- Mali-Burkina Faso Scoring Question Reaches Seventeen Days
sectorAlerts.headline: The open question of whether Mali and Burkina Faso are scored consistently for comparable conduct is now the longest-running item the benchmark is tracking.
observations: Mali (12.5 of 100): the Bamako siege continues, with convoys attacked and drivers kidnapped; not a new escalation.
Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100): not assessed tonight; the calibration question against Mali remains open.
The question has now run seventeen consecutive days without a decision, with no resolution timeline set.

methodologyNotes.name: A First Individual Review Can Correct a Placeholder Score by More Than Ten Points
methodologyNotes.description: Tunisia's published score of 34.4 was a placeholder from the benchmark's original bulk data import -- an estimate, not a score checked against Tunisia's own record. Tonight's first individual review found the evidence supports 23.8 instead, a gap of 10.6 points. The benchmark treats this differently from a country whose government got worse: the conduct behind Tunisia's lower reading -- the mass expulsions, the mass trials -- mostly predates tonight. What changed is that someone finally checked the placeholder against the record. The published score stays at 34.4 until the benchmark's editors act on the proposal.

methodologyNotes.name: A Score That Is Already at Zero Can Still Record a More Serious Finding
methodologyNotes.description: Israel, Sudan, and Russia all score 0 of 100, the lowest possible mark. Tonight's evidence -- Israel's expanding territorial control in Gaza, a UN warning about a possible new siege in Sudan, and Russia's latest strikes on Ukrainian civilians -- cannot lower any of these scores further, because there is no lower number to give. The benchmark records this evidence as confirming, or in Israel's case specifically reinforcing, the existing floor score rather than as a new downgrade, so the public record shows the evidence without implying a score change that cannot happen.

methodologyNotes.name: Two Data-Tracking Corrections Fixed Before Tonight's Assessment
methodologyNotes.description: Tunisia's internal working score had drifted to 32.4 in a slightly different rank position, but the correct, published score has been 34.4 all along. Somalia's internal rank had drifted to 187 instead of the correct 179. Both were caught and corrected before tonight's assessment began: the live, published score is always the authoritative one, and Tunisia's proposed 23.8 has been recorded only as a pending proposal, never written into the internal tracking record as if it were already the published score.

methodologyNotes.name: A Score Change Can Stay Within the Same Performance Band
methodologyNotes.description: Not every proposed score change moves a country or company into a different performance band. Tunisia's proposed drop from 34.4 to 23.8 is a real, evidence-based correction of 10.6 points, but both numbers fall inside the same Developing band (20 to 40 of 100). The benchmark treats a same-band correction and a band-crossing correction as different kinds of findings, since only the second changes which broad category the public sees an entity placed in.

forwardTriggers.trigger: United States-Iran talks resume in Doha. A verifiable agreement would be a positive scored event; a breakdown combined with new provocation would be a negative one.
Full reassessment due. Reversing the June 8 impunity law and releasing detained union leaders are the conditions that would move the score.
The natural seasonal peak for the current Ebola outbreak closes. Documented government obstruction of WHO response corridors is the trigger toward the absolute floor.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. A second undisclosed telemetry episode or a regulator finding of deceptive practice would be the next event that could move the score.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. No public compliance roadmap has been published yet.
The famine-risk window closes. A formal famine declaration combined with documented government aid obstruction would move the score.
The 2027 election under the July 2025 indefinite re-election amendment. Restoring due process and reinstating civil society are the conditions that would move the score back up.

The benchmark's editors act on the proposed 23.8 of 100 downgrade. Release of detained rights defenders or reinstatement of the shuttered information-access authority would be conditions for an upgrade instead.
Coordinator-level review of Mali's score against Burkina Faso's for comparable military-government conduct. Seventeen days open with no set review date.
The same coordinator-level review, from Burkina Faso's side of the calibration question.
First confirmed enforcement action under the new Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad.
An implemented ceasefire with unobstructed humanitarian access at El Obeid is the only path to a positive scored event from the absolute floor.
Independent verification of the July 10 prisoner exchange's completion and scale is the next event that would allow a scored assessment.
Independent confirmation of the forensic pathologist's undercount allegation, or documented aid obstruction, is the next event that would move the score toward a formal change from 18.0 of 100.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: Tunisia's proposed 23.8 and Turkey's published 17.6 both rest on documented judicial-dismantling conduct; would acting on Tunisia's Accountability-driven downgrade this cycle hold the two cases at a comparable distance, or does the gap constitute its own calibration question?
